Assignment 3: Employment-Based Green Card Sponsorship Analysis¶

An analysis of 147,000+ employer filings from Fiscal Year 2025, looking at who actually gets sponsored, for what jobs, and how much they get paid¶

Name: Yanjun Tan
Email: yjtan@umich.edu
GitHub: https://github.com/cloudygreennn/assignment-3/
Dashboard on Google Colab: https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1bTtYtCJL68bdL89TMAq2aK-uq0Lp1uKM?usp=sharing
Full Notebook on Google Colab: https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1_lA8uxC8US09y7JRLS7qlC7ayXE8Uh0b#scrollTo=9b8ccbe7-01f1-485b-8dff-60fb456d1be8

Deployment Instructions¶

This dashboard was developed and cleaned in Vocareum, and is also available on Google Colab for public access. Both environments run a live Python kernel and support ipywidgets interactivity natively, unlike static options like GitHub Pages.

Primary Deployment: Vocareum (Most Stable)

Vocareum Notebook: https://labs.vocareum.com/main/main.php?m=clabide&mode=s&asnid=5516395&stepid=5516396&hideNavBar=1

This is where the notebook was built and tested, and rendering has been the most consistent here. The environment should include 3 files:

  • assignment3.ipynb: https://vocproxy-1-30.us-west-2.vocareum.com/lab/tree/work/assignment3.ipynb
  • perm_data.csv: https://vocproxy-1-30.us-west-2.vocareum.com/lab/tree/work/perm_data.csv
  • df_clean.csv: https://vocproxy-1-30.us-west-2.vocareum.com/lab/tree/work/df_clean.csv

To run:

  1. Confirm all 3 files above are in the working directory
  2. Run all cells in assignment3.ipynb
  3. The interactive dashboard (3 dropdown controls + 6 linked charts) appears in Section 3.4

Note: Plotly charts occasionally render at an unexpected size, like resizing to half the screen or clustering together. If that happens, re-run the notebook, or re-run the affected cell individually to fix the chart size.

Also Available: Google Colab (Public Links)

  • Full Notebook (Code, Notes & Dashboard): https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1_lA8uxC8US09y7JRLS7qlC7ayXE8Uh0b
  • Dashboard Only Version: https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1bTtYtCJL68bdL89TMAq2aK-uq0Lp1uKM

To view and run on Colab:

  1. Open either link above
  2. Click Runtime → Run all to execute the whole notebook top to bottom
  3. Once everything finishes running, the interactive dashboard shows up at the bottom, with three dropdown filters (State, Company Size, Job Function) driving all six charts at once

Known issue on Colab (RESOLVED):

  • The final dashboard's rendering is not always stable.
  • Each of the 6 plots renders correctly when tested individually, but when combined into the final linked dashboard, charts may flicker, disappear, or render only partially on some runs.
  • Adding google.colab.output.enable_custom_widget_manager(), time.sleep(1.5), output.no_vertical_scroll() all did NOT fully resolve this.
  • If you experience this, the Vocareum link above is the recommended environment for a stable view of the dashboard.

Note: This issue has been resolved. The section below is kept here for reflection purposes, see the full debugging writeup in Problems Met & Reflection.

GitHub Repository

Repo link: https://github.com/cloudygreennn/assignment-3/tree/main

The repository includes:

  • The full .ipynb file (source code, notes, and final dashboard, as downloaded from Vocareum)
  • The cleaned dataset (df_clean.csv)
  • A README

Note: The original raw dataset is too large to include in this repository and is not uploaded here.

To reproduce this deployment locally:

  1. Clone or download this GitHub repo
  2. Open assignment3.ipynb in Jupyter, Colab, or another notebook environment of your choice
  3. Ensure df_clean.csv is either in the same working directory or read directly from its GitHub raw URL, as set up in the data loading cell
  4. Run all cells

No additional local installation is required beyond what's installed automatically in the first code cell of this notebook.

Data Introduction & Background¶

For Assignment 3, I'm looking at employment-based green card sponsorship in the U.S., using PERM data the Department of Labor released for fiscal year 2025.

PERM stands for Program Electronic Review Management, the first real step in most employment-based green card cases. It boils down to the employer proving to the government they tried to hire a qualified U.S. worker and couldn't find one, which is what opens the door for a foreign worker to fill the role instead.

I picked this topic because me and a lot of my friends are going through the process right now. :)

It's gotten harder to get a green card through employment sponsorship lately, for a few reasons. Fewer employers want to take on this lengthy, unpredictable process in the first place. The wage floor the Department of Labor sets for each job function climbs every year, raising the bar for what a foreign worker needs to be offered. Filing fees, admin costs, and rejection/RFE rates are all up too, scaring off employers who might otherwise take the risk.

So for this project, I wanted to dig into the real PERM data myself. Questions I kept coming back to:

  • How does the wage offered to sponsor someone differ by company size?
  • Do wages differ much across occupation types?
  • Which job titles show up most in PERM filings, and where geographically?
  • Does a company's age relate to what it pays, or how often it sponsors at all?
  • Does processing time relate to whether a case gets certified or denied?

Getting approved depends on more than any one factor, education, wage, job type, and required experience all play in. But the data still gives a decent window into these patterns.

What's in the data: this public disclosure dataset has 137 columns and 147,056 rows. Each row is one real PERM application filed by an employer sponsoring a foreign worker for an employment-based green card during Fiscal Year 2025 (10/1/2024 through 9/30/2025). Each row covers the employer (business name, industry code, number of employees, year established), the attorney or agent representing them if there is one, the job itself (title, SOC occupation code, offered wage, occupation type), the prevailing wage determination number, the worksite location (city, county, state), details on how the employer recruited (job order dates, ads placed, methods used), and how the case turned out (status, dates received and decided).

Data source: DOL Office of Foreign Labor Certification

Part 1: Visualization Technique(25%)¶

  1. Box Plots: Green Card Applicant's Wage Distribution by Company Size
  2. Violin Plots: Green Card Applicant's Wage Distribution by Company Age
  3. Histogram: PERM Wage Distribution by Occupation Type (Professional Occupation vs. Non-Professional Occupation)
  4. Bar Chart: Top 10 Employment-Based Green Card Applicant Job Titles
  5. Tree Map: PERM Employer Location by City (Top 20)
  6. Strip Plot: Processing Days by Case Outcome

1. Box Plots: Green Card Applicant's Wage Distribution by Company Size¶

In this case, we're using box plots to compare how wage distributions differ across different company sizes. Box plots work well here because we're interested in comparing distributions across categories, not just averages.

Using box plots lets us read off center, spread, skew, and any outliers side by side across all six groups at a glance:

  • Center line: the median wage for each group
  • Box height: the spread of the middle 50% of wages
  • Single dots above/below the whiskers: any outliers with unusually high or low wage offers
  • Overall shape: whether wages are tightly clustered or widely spread, and how many extreme outliers each group contains

Using box plots as the visualization technique here makes it easy to answer questions like:

  • Do larger companies generally pay more to sponsor a foreign worker for a Green Card?
  • How consistent are wages within each company size category?

Compared to other visualization techniques, like a bar chart which only compares averages, box plots reveal differences in both wage level and wage variability, which meets our needs better here.

2. Violin Plots: Green Card Applicant's Wage Distribution by Company Age¶

A violin plot sombines a box plot with with a mirrored density curve. It allows us to compare both summary statistics and the overall shape of the wage distribution. In this visualization, the embedded box plot shows the median and interquartile range, while the surrounding density curve highlights where wages are most concentrated.

  • Width at any point: how many applicants fall at that wage level. Wider sections indicate higher density.
  • Embedded box plots: the median and spread of the middle 50% of wages.
  • Overall silhouette: whether wages cluster tightly around a single value, spread evenly, or have multiple common wage points (a violin with more than one peak)

Using a violin plot here helps answer questions like:

  • Do older companies pay more consistently than newer ones, or just differently on average?
  • Does each age group have a single common wage range, or are there multiple wage clusters?
  • How does the overall wage distribution change as companies age??

Compared to a box plot, which only shows summary statistics (median, quartiles, outliers), a violin plot reveals the full density shape of the distribution. Since the previous visualization focuses on box plots, using a violin plot here provides additional insight into how wage distributions differ across company age groups.

3. Histogram: PERM Wage Distribution by Occupation Type (Professional Occupation vs. Non-Professional Occupation)¶

A stacked histogram works well to answer this question, because we want to learn both the wage distribution and the occupation breakdown at the same time. A stacked histogram shows:

  • Overall bar height: the total number of applications at that wage level, across all occupation types combined
  • Colored segments within each bar: how much of that total comes from each occupation type (Professional occupation, Non-professional, or College/University Teacher)

Using a stacked histogram here makes it easy to answer questions like:

  • Where does the bulk of PERM wage offers fall, regardless of occupation type?
  • Do professional occupations dominate over the others?

Unlike the box and violin plots used in Plots 1 and 2, which show an overall summary-statistics view, a histogram represents the full and granular shape of the distribution using raw record counts. It shows exactly how many applicants fall into each wage bracket. This makes it a genuinely different technique from the previous two charts.

4. Bar Chart: Top 10 Employment-Based Green Card Applicant Job Titles¶

A bar chart is one of the most straightforward visualization techniques for this kind of question. I picked a bar chart to answer "top 10..." type questions because it matches our need best: when the goal is simply to rank categories by count, a bar chart communicate the most directly with its bar length:

  • Bar length: directly shows how many applicants applied for a green card under that job title, making it immediately clear which roles appear most often in the dataset
  • Horizontal orientation: makes even very long job title labels easy to read without rotating or truncating text
  • Sorted order: ranking bars from longest to shortest turns the chart into an instant leaderboard

Using a bar chart here makes it easy to answer questions like:

  • Which job titles see the most PERM sponsorship activity?

Overall, a bar chart is a straightforward ranking of counts across a categorical variable.

5. Tree Map: PERM Employer Location by City (Top 20)¶

Tree maps show hierarchical data as nested rectangles. I picked this one partly because it was covered in this week's Module 3. Each rectangle size maps to volume (number of applications), and color here is just used to tell one city apart from the next, it's not tied to a second metric.

This works well for a "where's the activity concentrated" kind of question:

  • Size of each rectangle: the bigger the box, the more applications came from that city
  • Comparing 20 cities side by side is a lot easier here than scrolling through a ranked list
  • State isn't shown as a separate layer since city names alone don't need it. But I added it in the hover tooltip instead, so the chart doesn't get crowded with an extra level of nesting

6. Scatterd Plot: Processing Days by Case Outcome¶

A scatterd plot fits here because we're comparing a continuous variable, processing days, against a categorical one, case outcome, the same basic comparison as the box and violin plots back in Plots 1 and 2. But this time every single case shows up as its own dot instead of getting folded into a median or a quartile.

  • Each dot: one PERM application, plotted by how many days it took from received date to decision
  • Jitter: spreads the dots left and right inside each category so they do not just stack into a straight line, which is what actually lets you see density instead of a solid bar
  • Lower opacity: with tens of thousands of points per group, dropping the opacity way down helps too, denser spots come out darker and sparse spots almost disappear, so the eye can still find where cases really cluster

This setup makes it easy to answer questions like:

  • Do certified and denied cases take about the same time, or does one resolve faster?
  • Does an outcome hide subgroups, like a batch of unusually fast denials, that a median would smooth over?

Compared to the box and violin plots in Plots 1 and 2, a strip plot keeps every point visible, a real tradeoff since at this sample size it needs jitter and low opacity just to stay readable, but nothing gets averaged away either.

Part 2: Visualization Library(25%)¶

The dashboard framework and libraries you're using, and why they're suitable for this visualization. For instance, who created it? Is it open source? How do you install it? It is important to clarify that you should not use Altair, Matplotlib or Seaborn for this assignment. These are already used (or very similar to libraries used) in MADS or are very similar to used libraries and will not enhance our knowledge.

A discussion of the general approach and limitations of this framework. For instance, is it declarative or procedural? Does it integrate with Jupyter? Why did you decide to use this framework (especially if there are other options)?

1. Plotly: for Building the Plots¶

  • Website: https://plotly.com/python/
  • Developer: Plotly Technologies Inc. (Montreal-based)
  • Open Source: Yes, MIT license
  • Free to use? Yes, Plotly for Python is free and open source
  • Integrates with Jupyter? Yes, works natively, calling .show() renders each figure as an interactive widget right in the output cell
  • Declarative or procedural? Declarative. You write code describing what the final result should look like, not step-by-step instructions on how to draw it. You specify the data, the x/y mapping, the grouping variable, and Plotly figures out how to actually draw it, which is different from Matplotlib's more step-by-step drawing commands. That's what makes rebuilding six charts on every dropdown change manageable, the update function just calls something like px.box(filtered_df, x="company_size_bin", y="annual_wage") again and gets a complete, correctly formatted chart back, no manual redraw logic needed.

Reference¶

https://plotly.com/python/getting-started/

Installation¶

  • Install with pip: pip install plotly
  • or conda: conda install -c conda-forge plotly
  • For Plotly Express specifically: pip install plotly[express]

Why Plotly?¶

Two main reasons I picked Plotly Express here are:

  1. it covers every chart type I needed, box plots, violin plots, histograms, bar charts, treemaps, strip plots.
  2. it's genuinely easy to use, no complicated custom code, and the output looks good by default, hover details, color palettes that look more polished than Matplotlib out of the box.

A few other reasons it made sense:

  • Low code effort for good-looking output, a lot comes built in
  • Renders inline in Jupyter with no real setup
  • Hover, zoom, pan all work by default, no extra config
  • Pairs cleanly with ipywidgets for the linked filtering in this dashboard, each chart just gets rebuilt inside the update function on the filtered data
  • Documentation and example gallery are solid, useful when a specific chart type wasn't obvious how to build

Some Limitations I Ran Into¶

  • Large datasets get dense fast. The strip plot in Plot 6 has tens of thousands of points, and the defaults made it unreadable, had to manually tune marker opacity and jitter to get any real density visible instead of a solid blob
  • Fine layout control needs workarounds. Things like the multi-line x-axis labels with sample counts in Plots 1 and 2 aren't a built-in option, had to manually generate tick labels instead of using the high-level API directly
  • No built-in dashboard state management. Plotly Express only handles individual charts. Linking three dropdowns to six charts at once had to be built separately with ipywidgets, Plotly alone doesn't coordinate multiple charts
  • Performance can slow down with large datasets. A lot of people online list this as a weakness, but with roughly 147,056 rows by 15 columns after cleaning, I didn't notice much of a slowdown myself. My notebook crashed a few times, but rendering the actual visualizations stayed quick

2. ipywidgets: for Building the Dashboard¶

  • Website: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/
  • Developer: Project Jupyter, the same team behind Jupyter Notebook
  • Open Source: Yes, BSD-3-Clause license
  • Free to use? Yes, free and open source
  • Integrates with Jupyter? Yes, natively, it's built specifically for Jupyter/IPython, no extra setup needed in Jupyter
  • Declarative or procedural? More procedural than Plotly. Instead of just describing what I want to see, I have to say what should happen step by step: create a dropdown, watch it for changes, and when the value changes, run a function that filters the data and redraws the chart. Plotly handles the "what it looks like" part, ipywidgets handles the "when to update it" part.

Reference¶

https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user_install.html

Installation¶

  • pip: pip install ipywidgets
  • conda: conda install -c conda-forge ipywidgets
  • Colab comes with it preinstalled already, no separate step needed

Why ipywidgets?¶

Because it's what Module 3 actually teaches, so following the steps from course resources and videos is much easier. It's also lightweight, three dropdowns and one update function is enough to filter the data and redraw all six charts, no separate app framework or server required.

Another reason: I found it much easier and more user-friendly compared to the other library introduced in Module 3, Panel.

Other reasons...

  • Works entirely inside the notebook
  • interact() handles the connection, connect a function's parameters to widget values and it builds the UI and binds the callback in one shot, easy
  • Runs natively in Colab, which made getting this dashboard online much simpler than building and hosting a standalone web app
  • Small learning curve, the widget-plus-observe pattern is easy enough to reason about without digging into a bigger framework

Some Limitations I Ran Into¶

  • No automatic grid layout. ipywidgets doesn't arrange multiple outputs into a grid by default, all six charts just stack vertically unless you build a layout manually with HBox/VBox, which I skipped given the time I had
  • Redraws everything on every change. Each time a dropdown changes, all six charts get cleared and rebuilt from scratch, no partial update, so I ended up adding a loading message so it doesn't look frozen mid-regeneration
  • Only works with a live Python kernel. The interactivity doesn't survive if the notebook gets exported to static HTML or viewed on GitHub, it only works while actually running, which is exactly why this dashboard needed to be deployed on Colab and not just committed as a file

Part 3: Demonstration (50%)¶

3.1 Intall Package & Import Data¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
from ipywidgets import interact, Dropdown
from IPython.display import clear_output, display, Markdown

pd.set_option('display.max_rows', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
In [2]:
### NOTE: this block was run once locally to download and save the raw data.
### it is not re-run during deployment to avoid the long download/parse time
### (the original file is ~87MB and takes several minutes to fetch and parse)
### the saved output is instead hosted externally and loaded directly in the cell below

df = pd.read_csv("perm_data.csv", low_memory=False)

2 G-200-23334-532174 Certified 2023-11-29 2025-04-05 Non-professional South Georgia Pecan Company NaN 2220 E Hill Ave NaN Valdosta GA 31601 UNITED STATES OF AMERICA NaN 12295887330 NaN 58-1533837 115114.0 190.0 1913.0 N N Brown Tocsha NaN HR Manager 2220 E Hill Ave NaN Valdosta GA 31601 UNITED STATES OF AMERICA NaN 12295887330 NaN hrdept@georgiapecan.com NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN Y N P-100-23058-806019 53-7064.00 Packers and Packagers, Hand Pecan Packers NaN 19635.0 19635.0 Year NaN Business Premises 309 S. Lee Street NaN Valdosta NaN GA 31601 Valdosta, GA N N Y N NaN NaN NaN N N NaN NaN N NaN NaN N N N N NaN N N N 2023-09-05 00:00:00 2023-10-06 00:00:00 Y The Valdosta Daily Times 2023-09-10 00:00:00 Newspaper of General Circulation The Valdosta Daily Times 2023-09-17 00:00:00 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN N Y N N N N Y NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN

Data Cleaning Process

  1. Dropped all columns with > 90% missing values: 27 columns gone, taking the dataframe from 137 columns down to 110.
  2. Dropped columns not needed for visualization, another 95 gone, down from 110 to 15.
  3. Calculated and normalized the new fields the plots need:
    • Normalize annual wage: the raw JOB_OPP_WAGE_FROM field wasn't on one consistent unit, some rows were Year, others Hour, Month, Bi-Weekly, or Week. Converted everything to an annualized number so wages could actually be compared.
    • Company size bins, based on EMP_NUM_PAYROLL: <50, 50-200, 200-500, 500-1000, 1000-5000, 5000+.
    • Company age, calculated as 2025 - EMP_YEAR_COMMENCED, then binned into <5 years, 5-20 years, 20-50 years, 50+ years.
    • Processing days, just DECISION_DATE - RECEIVED_DATE.
    • Recategorized case status into Certified, Denied, or Withdrawn. The raw data actually splits certified into two separate values, "Certified" and "Certified - Expired", so those got merged into one Certified group.
    • Cleaned up city names in PRIMARY_WORKSITE_CITY, trimmed extra whitespace and made capitalization consistent so the same city doesn't end up split across a few different spellings.
In [6]:
# 1. DROP ALL COLUMNS WITH > 90% MISSING VALUE

# check missing value percent in each column
na_pct = df.isna().mean()

# locate columns with more than 90% missing values
threshold = 0.90
cols_to_drop = na_pct[na_pct > threshold].index.tolist()

# drop columns
df_clean_1 = df.drop(columns=cols_to_drop)

print("Before:", df.shape)
print("After:", df_clean_1.shape)

In [7]:
# 2. DROP COLUMNS I DON'T NEED FOR FINAL DASHBOARD

# keep only the columns required by the 3 controls + 6 plots
columns_needed = [
    # 3 controls
    "PRIMARY_WORKSITE_STATE",     # control 1: state
    "EMP_NUM_PAYROLL",            # control 2: company size (use for size bin)
    "PWD_SOC_TITLE",              # control 3: job function

    # plot 1: box plot 
    # company size bin vs. wage
    "JOB_OPP_WAGE_FROM",
    "JOB_OPP_WAGE_TO",
    "JOB_OPP_WAGE_PER",

    # plot 2: violin plot
    # company age bin vs. wage
    "EMP_YEAR_COMMENCED",        

    # plot 3: histogram
    # wage distribution by occupation type
    "OCCUPATION_TYPE",

    # plot 4: bar chart
    # top 10 job titles by selected states / company size / job function
    "JOB_TITLE",

    # plot 5: treemap (city distribution)
    "PRIMARY_WORKSITE_CITY",

    # plot 6: scatter (certified vs not, processing days)
    "CASE_STATUS",
    "RECEIVED_DATE",
    "DECISION_DATE",

    # for reference
    "CASE_NUMBER",
    "EMP_BUSINESS_NAME"
]

df_clean = df_clean_1[columns_needed].copy()

print("Before:", df_clean_1.shape)
print("After:", df_clean.shape)

Before: (147056, 110)
After: (147056, 15)

In [9]:
# 3. CALCULATE / NORMALIZE NEW FIELDS NEEDED FOR THE PLOTS

# 3.1 normalize annual wage data
# current wage field "JOB_OPP_WAGE_FROM" is not on the same unit
# df_clean['JOB_OPP_WAGE_PER'].unique()
# array(['Year', 'Hour', 'Month', 'Bi-Weekly', 'Week', nan], dtype=object)

# assuming full time job = 40 hrs/week, 52 weeks/year
def annualize_wage(row):
    rate = row["JOB_OPP_WAGE_FROM"]
    unit = row["JOB_OPP_WAGE_PER"]
    
    if pd.isna(rate) or pd.isna(unit):
        return None
    if unit == "Hour":
        return rate * 2080 
    elif unit == "Week":
        return rate * 52
    elif unit == "Bi-Weekly":
        return rate * 26
    elif unit == "Month":
        return rate * 12
    elif unit == "Year":
        return rate
    else:
        return None

df_clean["annual_wage"] = df_clean.apply(annualize_wage, axis=1)


# 3.2 set company size bins

# check company size data value distrobution

_ = '''
INPUT:
df_clean["EMP_NUM_PAYROLL"].describe().round()

OUTPUT:
count    147042.0
mean       5024.0
std       18572.0
min           0.0
25%          47.0
50%         344.0
75%        1944.0
max      936464.0
'''

df_clean["company_size_bin"] = pd.cut(
    df_clean["EMP_NUM_PAYROLL"],
    bins=[0, 50, 200, 500, 1000, 5000, float("inf")],
    labels=["<50", "50-200", "200-500", "500-1000", "1000-5000", "5000+"],
    include_lowest=True
)


# 3.3 set company year of est. bins
CURRENT_YEAR = 2025
df_clean["company_age"] = CURRENT_YEAR - df_clean["EMP_YEAR_COMMENCED"]
df_clean["company_age_bin"] = pd.cut(
    df_clean["company_age"],
    bins=[0, 5, 20, 50, float("inf")],
    labels=["<5 years", "5-20 years", "20-50 years", "50+ years"],
    include_lowest=True
)

# 3.4 calculate processing days (decision date - recieved date)
df_clean["RECEIVED_DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(df_clean["RECEIVED_DATE"])
df_clean["DECISION_DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(df_clean["DECISION_DATE"])

df_clean["processing_days"] = (df_clean["DECISION_DATE"] - df_clean["RECEIVED_DATE"]).dt.days


# 3.5 group case status into Certified / Denied / Withdrawn
# because it has two value for Certified status: Certified" and "Certified - Expired"
def categorize_status(status):
    if status in ["Certified", "Certified - Expired"]:
        return "Certified"
    elif status == "Denied":
        return "Denied"
    elif status == "Withdrawn":
        return "Withdrawn"
    else:
        return "Other"
df_clean["certification_group"] = df_clean["CASE_STATUS"].apply(categorize_status)

# 3.6 update format for city names
df_clean["city_clean"] = df_clean["PRIMARY_WORKSITE_CITY"].str.strip().str.title()
In [10]:
# UPDATE FOR 3.1 normalize annual wage data
# fix wage data who mistakely put "hour" as unit to their annual salary
# detect rows where the unit says "Hour" but the raw value is clearly already an annual figure
# nobody's hourly wage is above ~$500/hr in this dataset's context

mislabeled_hourly = (
    (df_clean["JOB_OPP_WAGE_PER"] == "Hour") & 
    (df_clean["JOB_OPP_WAGE_FROM"] > 500)
)

# for these rows, treat the raw value as already annual, not hourly
df_clean.loc[mislabeled_hourly, "annual_wage"] = df_clean.loc[mislabeled_hourly, "JOB_OPP_WAGE_FROM"]

# then apply the general sanity filter to catch anything still unreasonable
df_clean.loc[
    (df_clean["annual_wage"] < 10000) | (df_clean["annual_wage"] > 1000000),
    "annual_wage"
] = None

# print(df_clean["annual_wage"].describe())
# MISLABELED HOURLY COUNT: 106
# count     146822.000000
# mean      100261.514535
# std        63237.431111
# min        12480.000000
# 25%        40061.000000
# 50%       100000.000000
# 75%       140000.000000
# max      1000000.000000
# Name: annual_wage, dtype: float64
# annual_wage values now seems more accurate

# save cleaned df as a .csv
df_clean.to_csv("df_clean.csv", index=False)

Box Plot: Do larger companies offer higher sponsorship wages?¶

In [18]:
# PLOT 1
# box plot: company size vs. wage

# calculate sample size per company size bin
group_counts_1 = (
    df_clean.dropna(subset=["company_size_bin", "annual_wage"])
    .groupby("company_size_bin", observed=True)
    .size()
)

# build x axis labels to include the count
label_map_1 = {
    bin_name: f"{bin_name}<br>(n={count:,})"
    for bin_name, count in group_counts_1.items()
}

fig1 = px.box(
    df_clean.dropna(subset=["company_size_bin", "annual_wage"]),
    x="company_size_bin",
    y="annual_wage",
    category_orders={"company_size_bin": ["<50", "50-200", "200-500", "500-1000", "1000-5000", "5000+"]},
    title="<b>PERM Wage Distribution by Company Size (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Do larger companies offer higher sponsorship wages to foreign workers?</sup>",
    labels={
        "company_size_bin": "Company Size (# of Employees)",
        "annual_wage": "Annual Wage (USD)"
    }
)

# apply new count labels to the x-axis
fig1.update_xaxes(
    tickmode="array",
    tickvals=[str(v) for v in group_counts_1.index],
    ticktext=[label_map_1[bin_name] for bin_name in group_counts_1.index]
)

fig1.update_layout(
    height=600,
    autosize=True,
    title_x=0.5,
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f"
)

overall_median = df_clean["annual_wage"].median()
fig1.add_hline(
    y=overall_median,
    line_dash="dash",
    line_color="red"
)
fig1.add_annotation(
    text=f"<span style='color:red'>┅</span> Overall median wage: ${overall_median:,.0f}",
    xref="paper", yref="paper",
    x=0.5, y=1.06,
    showarrow=False,
    font=dict(size=13, color="gray"),
    align="left"
)

fig1.show(config={"responsive": True})

Takeaway from Plot 1: Larger companies pay more, and more consistently, but small companies drive most of the volume¶

From the box plots, we can tell that larger companies tend to offer higher and more consistent wages when sponsoring foreign workers for employment-based green cards. Smaller companies, by contrast, have much greater wage variation, ranging from the lowest wages to some of the most extreme highest outliers in the dataset. Besides, the plot reveals the companies with <50 employees actually account for the largest share of PERM applications (n=38,077), it's more than any other group, suggesting that most PERM sponsorship activity comes not from large employers, but from a high volume of small companies.

Violin Plot: Are older, more established companies more likely to sponsor foreign workers?¶

In [19]:
# PLOT 2
# violin plot: company age bin vs wage

# calculate sample size per group
group_counts_2 = (
    df_clean.dropna(subset=["company_age_bin", "annual_wage"])
    .groupby("company_age_bin", observed=True)
    .size()
)

# build new x-axis labels that include the count above
label_map_2 = {
    bin_name: f"{bin_name}<br>(n={count:,})"
    for bin_name, count in group_counts_2.items()
}

fig2 = px.violin(
    df_clean.dropna(subset=["company_age_bin", "annual_wage"]),
    x="company_age_bin",
    y="annual_wage",
    box=True,
    points=False,
    category_orders={"company_age_bin": ["<5 years", "5-20 years", "20-50 years", "50+ years"]},
    title="<b>PERM Wage Distribution by Company Age (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Are older, more established companies more likely to sponsor foreign workers?</sup>",
    labels={
        "company_age_bin": "Company Age (Years in Business)",
        "annual_wage": "Annual Wage (USD)"
    }
)

# add the count-included labels to the x-axis
fig2.update_xaxes(
    tickmode="array",
    tickvals=[str(v) for v in group_counts_2.index],
    ticktext=[label_map_2[bin_name] for bin_name in group_counts_2.index]
)

fig2.update_layout(
    height=600,
    autosize=True,
    title_x=0.5,
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f"
)

fig2.show(config={"responsive": True})

Takeaway from Plot 2: Company age matters for wages, but far less than company size does¶

Older companies tend to offer somewhat higher and more consistent wages than younger ones, though the effect is weaker than the one seen for company size. Companies of every age show pretty much the same long tail of high-wage outliers. Companies aged 5-50 years (the middle two groups) account for the vast majority of PERM applications, while companies under 5 years old show the widest wage variation despite offering similar median wages compared to those older firms. This suggests that wages for younger companies are likely negotiated case by case rather than following an established pay structure.

Histogram: Do foreign workers with professional occupations command higher sponsorship wages?¶

In [20]:
# PLOT 3
# histogram: wage distribution by occupation type

fig3 = px.histogram(
    df_clean.dropna(subset=["annual_wage", "OCCUPATION_TYPE"]),
    x="annual_wage",
    color="OCCUPATION_TYPE",
    nbins=20,  
    title="<b>PERM Wage Distribution by Occupation Type (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Do professional occupations command higher sponsorship wages?</sup>",
    labels={"annual_wage": "Annual Wage (USD)"}
)

fig3.update_layout(
    yaxis_title="Number of Applications",
    height=600,
    autosize=True,
    title_x=0.5,
    xaxis_tickformat="$,.0f",
    bargap=0.1,
    xaxis=dict(
        tickmode="linear",
        dtick=40000,       
        tickangle=45   
    )
)

fig3.show(config={"responsive": True})

Takeaway from Plot 3: A sharp $80,000 divide separates professional and non-professional wages¶

Majority of non-professional occupation applicants' wages are below the $80,000 threshold, while professional occupations account for nearly all applications above it. This suggests that occupation type is one of the strongest drivers of wage level among green card applicants.

Bar Chart: Which jobs are most likely to get sponsored by employers?¶

In [21]:
# PLOT 4
# bar chart: top 10 job titles
top_titles = df_clean["JOB_TITLE"].value_counts().head(10).reset_index()
top_titles.columns = ["JOB_TITLE", "count"]

fig4 = px.bar(
    top_titles,
    x="count",
    y="JOB_TITLE",
    orientation="h",
    title="<b>Top 10 Employment-Based Green Card Applicant Job Titles (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Which jobs are most likely to get sponsored?</sup>",
    labels={"JOB_TITLE": "Job Title"}
)
fig4.update_layout(
    yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"},
    title_x=0.5,
    height=600,
    autosize=True,
    xaxis_title="# of Applications",
)
fig4.show(config={"responsive": True})

Takeaway from Plot 4: Software Engineer dominates the top 10 sponsored job titles¶

Software Engineer is by far the most common job title among PERM applicants in FY2025, at more than double the volume of any other role. The top 10 splits into two worlds: tech/medical roles (Software Engineer, Software Developer, Senior Software Engineer, Medical Technologist) and manual food-processing jobs (Crew Member, Meat Cutter and Trimmer, Fish Cutter and Trimmer, Pecan Packers, Poultry Processors).

Treemap: Where are foreign workers most likely to find a green-card-sponsoring employer?¶

In [22]:
# PLOT 5
# treemap: PERM job city distribution (state shown on hover only)

top_cities = (
    df_clean.dropna(subset=["PRIMARY_WORKSITE_STATE", "city_clean"])
    .groupby(["PRIMARY_WORKSITE_STATE", "city_clean"])
    .size()
    .reset_index(name="count")
    .nlargest(20, "count")
)

fig5 = px.treemap(
    top_cities,
    path=["city_clean"],          
    values="count",
    custom_data=["PRIMARY_WORKSITE_STATE"],  # add state info on hover tag
    title="<b>TOP 20 PERM Employer Location by City (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Where are foreign workers most likely to find a green-card-sponsoring employer?</sup>",
    labels={"city_clean": "City", "count": "Number of Applications"}
)

fig5.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="<b>%{label}</b><br>State: %{customdata[0]}<br>Applications: %{value}<extra></extra>"
)

fig5.update_layout(
    title_x=0.5,
    height=600,
    autosize=True
)

fig5.show(config={"responsive": True})

Takeaway from Plot 5: Sponsorship concentrates in tech hubs, with a few small-town outliers tied to food processing¶

PERM sponsorship clusters hard around major tech hubs, New York leading the pack. But a couple of small Georgia towns, Thomasville and Eutaw, punch way above their population size, probably tied to food-processing employers. Fits right in with the manual-labor job titles we saw in Plot 4.

Scatterd Plot: Does how long an application takes relate to whether it gets approved?¶

In [23]:
# plot 6
# scattered plot for processing dataes vs. case status

fig6 = px.strip(
    df_clean.dropna(subset=["certification_group", "processing_days"]),
    x="certification_group",
    y="processing_days",
    color="certification_group",
    title="<b>Processing Days by Case Outcome (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Does how long an application takes relate to whether it gets approved?</sup>",
    labels={"certification_group": "Case Status", "processing_days": "Processing Days"}
)

fig6.update_layout(
    height=600,
    autosize=True,
    title_x=0.5
)

fig6.update_traces(
    marker=dict(size=4, opacity=0.2),
    jitter=0.8
)

fig6.show(config={"responsive": True})

Takeaway from Plot 6: Processing time doesn't strongly predict case outcome¶

Certified and Denied cases both center around 400 to 600 days of processing, though Denied includes a cluster of much faster rejections, while Withdrawn cases show no meaningful clustering at all, consistent with withdrawal being an employer's own decision rather than a DOL determination tied to a typical review timeline.

3.3 Interactive Dashboard¶

Access Dashboard on Google Colab: https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1bTtYtCJL68bdL89TMAq2aK-uq0Lp1uKM?usp=sharing

In [24]:
from ipywidgets import interact, Dropdown
from IPython.display import clear_output, display, Markdown
import plotly.express as px

# build dropdown options from the cleaned data
state_options = ["All"] + sorted(df_clean["PRIMARY_WORKSITE_STATE"].dropna().unique().tolist())
size_options = ["All", "<50", "50-200", "200-500", "500-1000", "1000-5000", "5000+"]
job_options = ["All"] + sorted(df_clean["PWD_SOC_TITLE"].dropna().unique().tolist())


def update_dashboard(state, size, job):
    clear_output(wait=True)
    display(Markdown(f"Dashboard updating in progress..."))

    # filter data based on current dropdown selections
    filtered_df = df_clean.copy()
    if state != "All":
        filtered_df = filtered_df[filtered_df["PRIMARY_WORKSITE_STATE"] == state]
    if size != "All":
        filtered_df = filtered_df[filtered_df["company_size_bin"] == size]
    if job != "All":
        filtered_df = filtered_df[filtered_df["PWD_SOC_TITLE"] == job]

    clear_output(wait=True)
    display(Markdown(f"---\n\n✅ **Dashboard Updated**: Showing **{len(filtered_df):,} applications** matching current filters"))

    # PLOT 1: box plot: company size vs. wage
    group_counts_1 = (
        filtered_df.dropna(subset=["company_size_bin", "annual_wage"])
        .groupby("company_size_bin", observed=True)
        .size()
    )
    label_map_1 = {
        bin_name: f"{bin_name}<br>(n={count:,})"
        for bin_name, count in group_counts_1.items()
    }
    fig1 = px.box(
        filtered_df.dropna(subset=["company_size_bin", "annual_wage"]),
        x="company_size_bin",
        y="annual_wage",
        category_orders={"company_size_bin": ["<50", "50-200", "200-500", "500-1000", "1000-5000", "5000+"]},
        title="<b>PERM Wage Distribution by Company Size (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Do larger companies offer higher sponsorship wages?</sup>",
        labels={
            "company_size_bin": "Company Size (# of Employees)",
            "annual_wage": "Annual Wage (USD)"
        }
    )
    fig1.update_xaxes(
        tickmode="array",
        tickvals=list(group_counts_1.index),
        ticktext=[label_map_1[bin_name] for bin_name in group_counts_1.index]
    )
    fig1.update_layout(
        height=600,
        title_x=0.5,
        yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f"
    )
    fig1.show()

    # PLOT 2: violin plot: company age bin vs wage
    group_counts_2 = (
        filtered_df.dropna(subset=["company_age_bin", "annual_wage"])
        .groupby("company_age_bin", observed=True)
        .size()
    )
    label_map_2 = {
        bin_name: f"{bin_name}<br>(n={count:,})"
        for bin_name, count in group_counts_2.items()
    }
    fig2 = px.violin(
        filtered_df.dropna(subset=["company_age_bin", "annual_wage"]),
        x="company_age_bin",
        y="annual_wage",
        box=True,
        points=False,
        category_orders={"company_age_bin": ["<5 years", "5-20 years", "20-50 years", "50+ years"]},
        title="<b>PERM Wage Distribution by Company Age (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Are older, more established companies more likely to sponsor foreign workers?</sup>",
        labels={
            "company_age_bin": "Company Age (Years in Business)",
            "annual_wage": "Annual Wage (USD)"
        }
    )
    fig2.update_xaxes(
        tickmode="array",
        tickvals=list(group_counts_2.index),
        ticktext=[label_map_2[bin_name] for bin_name in group_counts_2.index]
    )
    fig2.update_layout(
        height=600,
        title_x=0.5,
        yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f"
    )
    fig2.show()

    # PLOT 3: histogram: wage distribution by occupation type
    fig3 = px.histogram(
        filtered_df.dropna(subset=["annual_wage", "OCCUPATION_TYPE"]),
        x="annual_wage",
        color="OCCUPATION_TYPE",
        title="<b>PERM Wage Distribution by Occupation Type (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Do professional occupations command higher sponsorship wages?</sup>",
        labels={"annual_wage": "Annual Wage (USD)"},
        category_orders={"OCCUPATION_TYPE": ["Professional occupation", "Non-professional", "College/University Teacher"]}
    )
    fig3.update_traces(xbins=dict(start=0, end=1000000, size=40000))
    fig3.update_layout(
        height=600,
        title_x=0.5,
        xaxis_tickformat="$,.0f",
        bargap=0.1,
        xaxis=dict(tickmode="linear", dtick=40000, tickangle=45),
        yaxis_title="Number of Applications"
    )
    fig3.show()

    # PLOT 4: bar chart: top 10 job titles
    top_titles = filtered_df["JOB_TITLE"].value_counts().head(10).reset_index()
    top_titles.columns = ["JOB_TITLE", "count"]
    fig4 = px.bar(
        top_titles,
        x="count",
        y="JOB_TITLE",
        orientation="h",
        title="<b>Top 10 Employment-Based Green Card Applicant Job Titles</b><br><sup>Which jobs are most likely to get sponsored?</sup>",
        labels={"JOB_TITLE": "Job Title"}
    )
    fig4.update_layout(
        yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"},
        title_x=0.5,
        height=600,
        xaxis_title="Number of Applications",
    )
    fig4.show()

    # PLOT 5: treemap: PERM job city distribution
    top_cities = (
        filtered_df.dropna(subset=["PRIMARY_WORKSITE_STATE", "city_clean"])
        .groupby(["PRIMARY_WORKSITE_STATE", "city_clean"])
        .size()
        .reset_index(name="count")
        .nlargest(20, "count")
    )
    fig5 = px.treemap(
        top_cities,
        path=["city_clean"],
        values="count",
        custom_data=["PRIMARY_WORKSITE_STATE"],
        title="<b>PERM Employer Location by City (Top 20)</b><br><sup>Where are you most likely to find a green-card-sponsoring employer?</sup>",
        labels={"city_clean": "City", "count": "Number of Applications"}
    )
    fig5.update_traces(
        hovertemplate="<b>%{label}</b><br>State: %{customdata[0]}<br>Applications: %{value}<extra></extra>"
    )
    fig5.update_layout(
        title_x=0.5,
        height=600
    )
    fig5.show()

    # PLOT 6: strip plot: processing days by case outcome
    plot6_df = filtered_df[
        filtered_df["certification_group"].isin(["Certified", "Denied"])
    ].dropna(subset=["processing_days"])

    fig6 = px.strip(
        plot6_df,
        x="certification_group",
        y="processing_days",
        color="certification_group",
        title="<b>Processing Days by Case Outcome (FY2025)</b><br><sup>Does how long an application takes relate to whether it gets approved?</sup>",
        labels={"certification_group": "Case Status", "processing_days": "Processing Days"}
    )
    fig6.update_traces(
        marker=dict(size=3, opacity=0.55),
        jitter=0.8
    )
    fig6.update_layout(
        height=600,
        title_x=0.5
    )
    fig6.show()


# connect the dropdowns to the update function
label_style = {"description_width": "120px", "font_weight": "bold"}

state_dropdown = Dropdown(
    options=state_options,
    description="State:",
    style=label_style,
    layout={"width": "250px"}
)
size_dropdown = Dropdown(
    options=size_options,
    description="Company Size:",
    style=label_style,
    layout={"width": "250px"}
)
job_dropdown = Dropdown(
    options=job_options,
    description="Job Function:",
    style=label_style,
    layout={"width": "500px"}
)

interact(
    update_dashboard,
    state=state_dropdown,
    size=size_dropdown,
    job=job_dropdown
)

interactive(children=(Dropdown(description='State:', layout=Layout(width='250px'), options=('All', 'AK', 'AL',…
Out[24]:
<function __main__.update_dashboard(state, size, job)>

Problems Met & Reflection¶

1. 3 hours of debugging later, it turned out to be a rendering race condition, not a Google Colab or Plotly bug¶

It took about 3 hours to debug why the final dashboard wouldn't render properly in Google Colab, and it turned out to be one of the trickier technical problems in this project, honestly more interesting than I expected.

Each of the 6 plots rendered fine when tested individually, but once combined into the final linked dashboard, charts would flicker, disappear, or only partially render. The pattern was inconsistent at first, sometimes only the first plot showed, sometimes only the last one, sometimes plots would appear and then vanish the moment the next one started rendering.

I tried a few fixes, roughly in this order:

  • google.colab.output.enable_custom_widget_manager(), to properly connect Plotly's rendering to Colab's widget system
  • output.no_vertical_scroll(), to stop Colab from clipping the output inside a fixed-height scrollable container
  • time.sleep(1.5) between each fig.show() call, to give the browser time to finish rendering one chart before starting the next

Each helped a little, none fully solved it. The real clue came from watching how the charts failed, whichever chart rendered last tended to stay stable, and everything rendered before it would disappear. That pointed to the actual cause: all six figures were getting pushed into the same shared output stream one after another, and each new .show() call forced Colab to recompute and redraw the entire output area, wiping out whatever had rendered just before it. Adding delays between charts didn't fix this, it just changed when the wipeout happened.

The fix was to stop sharing one output stream entirely. I created six separate ipywidgets.Output() containers, one per chart, so each figure renders into its own isolated space instead of competing for the same one. Combined with switching from interact() to manually binding .observe() on each dropdown, this finally gave a stable dashboard, no more flickering, no more charts disappearing on scroll.

Takeaway: the bug wasn't really about Plotly, Colab, or ipywidgets individually, it was a race condition in how repeated output calls competed for the same rendering space. Isolating each chart into its own container removed the competition instead of trying to manage the timing around it.

2. Finding real, reliable individual-level data source is harder than picking a dataset off Kaggle¶

Finding quality data, especially individual-level records, turned out to be a real pain. I could've just grabbed something off Kaggle, but I wanted first-hand data from a reliable source for this one, and that was way harder than expected. A few topics I tried before landing here:

  • Air Quality Data: with the recent Canadian wildfire smoke, I got curious about Michigan's air quality history. Found something from the EPA, but it only had a handful of columns, lat/long and air quality readings, not enough for what this assignment needed. Getting more would've meant stitching together multiple sources, and I wasn't even sure the extra data existed publicly. Gave up on this one.
  • Ann Arbor Parking Violations: the city has a public data portal, but pulling actual ticketing data (location, time, car model) requires a formal request that can take weeks to get approved. Not happening on this timeline.
  • Farmer Suicide Rates: remembered a news story on this, saying farmer's suicide rate is extremely high in the U.S., so wanted to check if raw data backed it up. Found CDC WONDER, a public hub with suicide data, but federal law only lets them release aggregate summaries, no individual records. Dead end.

Long story short, finding good, individual-level, publicly available data is nowhere near as easy as it sounds. :(

3. Cleaning real-world data means fixing entry mistakes, not just dropping them¶

Even in something as official as a green card filing, people mess up. This dataset was cleaner than most I looked at, but a few things still needed fixing.

Plot 1 (Company Size vs. Wage) is the clearest example. After plotting the raw numbers, some wages showed up over $500,000,000, obviously not real. Pulling the top outliers with df_clean.nlargest(20, "annual_wage")[["JOB_OPP_WAGE_FROM", "JOB_OPP_WAGE_PER", "annual_wage", "JOB_TITLE"]] showed most of them were senior or manager titles.

My best guess: someone meant to file an annual salary but picked "Hour" as the pay unit by mistake. Run that through my annualizing function and a normal $150k salary turns into $150k x 2,080 hours, well over $300 million. Instead of tossing those rows, since the underlying data was still good, I wrote a separate function to catch and fix that specific pattern. A few smaller formatting issues popped up too, nothing major, overall the data held up well.

4. Designing multiple charts around shared controls is a harder problem than writing the plot code itself¶

Writing the plot code was only half of this. Designing the dashboard itself was its own skill nobody warns you about. The six charts couldn't just be picked at random, they all had to actually respond to the same three controls, State, Company Size, Job Function. I had other chart ideas and side topics I wanted to dig into, but once I built them out, some just didn't play nice with the shared controls, so they got cut.
